data.title: Valero Energy: Solid Q4 Is Reflected In Valuation
data.description:
Shares of Valero Energy (VLO) have been a strong performer over the past year, gaining 35% as the refining macro environment is considerably better than 12 months ago. Ongoing capital returns have also supported shares. The company reported another strong quarter on Thursday morning, sending shares up another 2%. I last covered Valero in December when I downgraded shares to a “Hold,” but with the stock gaining another 7% since then, I should have kept them a “Buy,” though they did first approach my $160 buy target before rebounding.

Regime change actions in Venezuela (which I did not foresee) have been a key driver of outperformance, as that may increase VLO’s access to low-cost heavy crude oil. With updated financials and new macro developments, now is a good time to revisit Valero.

In the company’s fourth quarter, Valero earned $3.82 per share, blowing past estimates by $0.55. Revenue fell by 1% to $30 billion, though this is a less relevant metric for refiners. Gasoline prices significantly impact revenue, but refineries make money on the spread between gasoline/diesel and crude oil rather than on their absolute price, which is why a focus on crack spreads and refining margins is more relevant than revenue and price levels.

Crack spread improvement drives better results

Its refining segment earned $1.7 billion in Q4, nearly quadrupling the $441 million earned last year. Throughput was strong at 3.1mbd, which was up about 4% from last year. Refining margins per barrel were up to $13.61 from $8.44 last year, given the more favorable crack spread environment. Partially offsetting this, operating expenses were up about 8% to $5.03. I do expect operating cost pressures to persist this year. Refining is an energy-intensive process, and as you can see below, electricity prices have surged, in part due to increased demand from data centers. That pressure is likely to persist at least through 2026.

Ultimately though, crack spreads (the differential between crude oil input costs and product output prices) are what drive the business. Spreads surged in the first half of Q4 past $30, then plunged back towards $20, and have more recently recovered. While crack spreads are volatile, the level of volatility we have seen over the past four months has been unusual. Still, as you can see over Q4, spreads were safely above levels seen last year, which is why refining profits were up so substantially.

The question for investors is what the future environment will look like. I have written previously that I view $25 as a “normal” long-term crack spread. I downgraded VLO to “Hold” late last year in part because spreads had gotten beyond this level. Crack spreads are driven by the relative supply/demand of crude vs. refined products. Over the past few months, we have seen gasoline inventories surge from very low levels to very high levels (even considering the seasonal buildup during Q4). As gasoline inventories surged, this put pressure on crack spreads.

Frankly, the moves in crack spreads in both directions were a bit exaggerated, and spreads are now at $25 again, a healthy level consistent with my long-term view. Given my view that the United States is unlikely to have a recession this year, I expect stable gasoline demand this year, which should limit inventory builds. Plus, refineries are doing more seasonal maintenance in Q1, which temporarily reduces supply. Finally, the incremental supply from Venezuela is likely playing a role in the rebound we are seeing and has certainly altered sentiment in the sector.

Venezuela is an incremental positive

Earlier this month, the Trump Administration deposed Venezuelan President Nicolas Maduro, leading to regime change there. Venezuelan oil production has fallen significantly over the past decade given U.S. sanctions and mismanagement. Friendlier relations could lead to incremental investment and more production, reducing crude oil prices and widening crack spreads. This is especially positive for U.S. refiners, which are built to process the cheaper, heavy crude that Venezuela produces. Now, there is still a massive amount of uncertainty as to what will happen here, and I expect upstream producers will be cautious before investing significant sums.

As such, most of the potential benefits of higher production are unlikely to be realized before 2028, and there is a risk that Venezuelan production never rises much. Still, there are near-term benefits as friendlier relations have shifted oil flows, with Venezuela selling more to the United States. Indeed, Valero purchased one of the first Venezuelan cargos at a sizable discount to benchmark prices. All else equal, actions in Venezuela should lift VLO’s refining margin, but the impact is unlikely to be more than $0.50/barrel in my view. This is an incremental, not a game-changing positive.

I would also note that Valero is in the process of closing its Benicia refinery in California, though it has delayed the closure and will continue to produce gasoline through April after working with the governor. Unique regulations isolate the California gasoline market, and they also make it harder to profitably run a refinery in the state. I have been assuming a total closure of the facility, and I do not view this extension as material to investors.

Overall, I expect the crack spread environment to average about $25 in 2026. That said, in the near term, I see risks skewed to the downside, given the elevated level of gasoline inventories currently. Still for the full year, this will be an improvement over average 2025 levels and could provide an incremental ~$2 billion of free cash flow and ~$6 in EPS to the ~$10.61 reported in full year 2025. This also assumes a normal hurricane season, which can impact Gulf Coast throughput, a negative for VLO, though that often also leads to temporarily wider crack spreads.

Its renewable diesel unit earned $92 million, down from $170 million last year, as increased supply has weighed on margins. On the other hand, ethanol operating income was up $97 million to $117 million. Combined, these smaller, “green” units contributed similar earnings as a year ago. Given the pressures farmers are facing from the trade war, President Trump has suggested his support for higher ethanol standards year-end to make up for lost exported demand. This would be a positive for Valero, likely increasing margins on its production. Still, I expect these units to have flattish results in 2026 relative to 2025, and I view refining as the core of Valero’s earnings power and valuation.

The balance sheet enables capital returns

The company maintains an excellent balance sheet with $4.7 billion of cash and $8.3 billion of debt. That leaves the company with less than 1x net leverage, and I expect VLO to use all free cash flow for buybacks and dividends. Indeed, during Q4, VLO returned $1.4 billion to shareholders, bringing full-year capital returns to $4 billion. Thanks to buybacks, its share count was down about 4% from last year. I expect a somewhat faster pace of repurchases in 2026. Earlier this month, Valero also increased its dividend by 6%, and shares now offer a 2.6% dividend yield.

Back in December, I saw the company having $17 of run-rate capital return capacity, and this remains my view. I expect a ~5% share count reduction this year, which leaves the company with about a 7.6% capital return yield. This is certainly not unattractive, though the refining sector has a generally cheap valuation. While a company like Marathon Petroleum (MPC) has less Venezuela benefit, I expect it to have a capital return above 11% this year.

Shares are a suitable "Hold"

With the lack of long-term capacity growth, I view refineries as the long-term “bottleneck” in the petroleum supply chain, a positive for long-term margins, and as such, I am positively predisposed towards the sector. However, long-term obsolescence concerns (given ongoing electrification and fuel efficiency gains) along with their cyclicality are likely to structurally limit multiple expansion.

Within this construct and relative to refining peers, I view Valero as fairly valued, especially as I am cautious on the ultimate magnitude of Venezuela benefits. Given capital returns, I view VLO as a suitable “Hold” in portfolios, but with gasoline inventories elevated and a premium multiple vs. MPC (my top pick in the sector), I see better relative opportunities and would want to see shares pull back into the mid-$170s before adding. For short-term traders, I am cautious on crack spreads over the next 1-2 months given the elevated level of inventories, and a turn lower could put some downside pressure on shares, which is why I believe a better entry point may appear. For longer-term investors, I do not view 1-2 month movements in crack spreads as a reason to buy/sell, and with its capital return capacity, VLO is a suitable hold over a 12-month time horizon.

data.title: Silver Is In The Middle Of Repricing, Not The Peak Of Repricing
data.description:
There have been a lot of warning bells against silver since the beginning of this year. The reweighing argument took me out of a $66 strike call option on SLV (SLV). I sold it at a profit, but in hindsight, that was liquidated too early. I also hold physical silver, and more recently, I have been thinking of building a larger position in the metal. With some time to kill on a long flight, I thought I’d evaluate whether that instinct was driven by something more durable in the setup.

Initially I went back to first principles, which in this exercise was to find things that drive silver prices.

Why Supply/Demand Deficits Are Not a Good Indicator for Price Movements?

In 2024, the Silver Institute published a report on the factors that determine the price of silver. Intuitively, it is easy to link industrial supply and demand as a key source of price movement. While this forms the dominant narrative, the correlation statistics from the report suggest price is driven mainly by investment and bullion setups.

A cursory look at the decade-long deficit/surplus trajectory actually supports this view. In the 1990s, prices remained depressed in the $4-$6/oz range despite persistent supply deficits. In the 2009-2011 period, prices surged despite surpluses. And more recently, supply deficits since the 2020s produced a price surge only in 2025. The price movements during these periods are better explained by investor behavior, as they tend to mop up supply during surpluses and sell holdings during deficits.

Now that I know that investment demand is the most important price mover, I thought I’d look at current configurations and find a range for the price of silver.

First Method: Gold-to-Silver Reversion Model

The gold-to-silver ratio (or GSR) is a relative valuation tool preferred even by institutional desks. Apart from the strong correlation seen between the metals, it negates the effect of investor interest in safe haven assets. As an example, both silver and gold climbed up as risk-off sentiments increased during the Davos summit. The GSR fell but not as dramatically as the increase in gold and silver prices.

Over the last year, the ratio has been on a dramatic descent from 100x in March 2025 to around 47x now. So, the easy money in the reversion trade has already been captured. To find the lower end of the range, I anchored silver prices to expected targets on gold and used the 50x multiple from the 80/50 heuristic. This comes out to around $120 if you consider that gold is within reach of its $6,000 target.

To find the higher end of the range, I applied the historical lows in the ratio to this gold price anchor. Obviously, the 1980s ratio would result in an outlandish target. But considering that even the 2011 ratio of 32x results in a $120 - $187.50 range, the setup for silver is still skewed to the upside.

GSR Ratio Gold anchor Silver target
1980 15 $6,000 $400.00
2011 32 $6,000 $187.50
2026 50 $6,000 $120.00

The Davos summit was still ongoing when I first came up with this range. At this point, I thought I had enough to go on and make a decision and also became a bit impatient reading a research paper for the second method. So I bought a call option on SLV again with a strike price of $89. I sold this at nearly 3.7 times my cost on the 26th of January. It is important to note that for continued exposure, the expiry on the option forces a sell and a buyback at an expiry with a later date. While it amplifies returns, it is also a very risky way to gain exposure to SLV. Regardless, I bought a $118 call the next day and again sold it at a ~55% profit.

Second Method: M2 Money Supply Catch-up Trade

There is a cointegration study that shows a statistically significant relationship between M2 money supply and gold prices over 50-year periods. The correlation is very low in the short term. The Silver Institute report mentioned earlier also cites silver’s correlation with gold being high only in the long term. These dual correlations (Gold–M2 and Silver–Gold) could mean silver acts like a coiled spring, ignoring money supply for years before violently repricing in a short window. This looks a lot like what is happening right now.

For this calculation, we must find the expansion factor or the ratio of the current money supply to the money supply at previous peaks. The question we are trying to answer is, “What is $50 in silver from 1980 worth in today’s dollars, after adjusting for money supply?"

As seen above, we get a range of $123 to $697. The physical shortage we saw in October 2025 is similar to the 1980s peak when the Hunt brothers tried to corner the market. However, considering that $123 is still at the lower end of the range, I think the warnings on the metal seem misplaced at this point in time. I am therefore maintaining a “buy” rating on silver.

Caveats and Conclusion

In parabolic moves like the one silver is making, exchanges often raise margin requirements. This can force traders to sell positions regardless of conviction. This can be very violent with 20%-30% shakeouts even if the long-term trend remains intact. I'd therefore caution investors to look into their risk profiles while buying exposure to this metal.

Against plenty of warning bells, I think the silver market is not just in a bull market but in a structural repricing event. I think investors should look at the current breakout as a secular shift with the $100 becoming a floor rather than a ceiling in the coming year. I intend to hold and add physical silver to ride volatility while using options to capture the likely explosive upside of M2 reversion.

data.title: The Humanoids Have Arrived! At CES 2026
data.description:
By now, the headline statistics have been well publicized. Goldman Sachs (GS) estimates annual humanoid robot shipments could reach one million units by the early to mid-2030s.1 Morgan Stanley (MS) goes further, projecting more than one billion humanoids deployed globally by 2050.2 The numbers are eye-popping - but at this year’s Consumer Electronics Show (CES) in Las Vegas, I saw something more important than forecasts. I saw the humanoid future moving out of research labs and into the real world.

CES is known to be a proving ground for technologies on the verge of mass adoption.3Whether it was the VCR in the 1970s, the CD player in the early 1980s, DVDs in the 1990s, or smart TVs and mobile computing in the 2000s, the show has a long track record of spotlighting innovations just before they enter daily life. This year, from what I saw, I believe humanoid robots fit that pattern.

Walking the halls of CES, I encountered dozens of new humanoid robot brands - alongside component manufacturers, data-collection platforms, operating systems, and embodied-intelligence software providers. One thing quickly became obvious: the epicenter of this ecosystem is China. As seen in the chart below, many humanoid exhibitors either came directly from China or were founded by Chinese engineers now operating in Silicon Valley or elsewhere. Mandarin was spoken as frequently as English. I believe this may be a sign of an improving geopolitical atmosphere, as management teams were eyeing the US market and were enthusiastic about their future business prospects here.

It was encouraging to see how closely the conference aligned with the investment framework of our KraneShares Global Humanoid and Embodied Intelligence ETF (KOID). KOID is designed to capture the broad humanoid value chain: the components, the brains, and the integrators building real-world humanoid systems across the U.S., China, Europe, and broader Asia. CES confirmed that this ecosystem is not theoretical - it is already forming at scale.

With that opportunity comes risk. KOID is focused on a fast-evolving, global technology ecosystem, investing in AI- and robotics-related companies across multiple geographies, including non-U.S. issuers and companies at earlier stages such as IPOs, which can experience elevated volatility. While the fund provides exposure across different regions and segments of the humanoid value chain, it is a concentrated, non-diversified strategy. As a result, adverse developments affecting a single company, sector, or theme within the portfolio may have an outsized impact on performance.

Integrators steal the show

The stars of the show were the integrators, the companies assembling and selling humanoid robot models.

Unitree’s now-famous G1 boxing robots drew massive crowds, as did athletic humanoid demonstrations from EngineAI, whose booth placement directly adjacent to Unitree felt overtly strategic. AgiBot - whom we visited in China last fall - showcased its full-sized A2 humanoid, which recently set a record for longest distance walked by a humanoid robot (106.29 km/66.04 miles).4 Alongside it were the smaller X1 and X2 models performing choreographed dance routines, plus a robotic panda that quickly became a crowd favorite. Noetix, another company we visited at its Beijing factory last year, let attendees physically kick and push its lightweight N2 humanoid to demonstrate balance and durability. Booster Robotics staged a classroom of synchronized T1 robots waving in unison. Galbot (also part of last fall’s visit) demonstrated its award-winning autonomous retail clerk (which won the medical scenario drug sorting competition at the World Humanoid Robot Games).5 Galbot is already operating in real-world store environments, such as its consumer goods stand "Galaxy Space Capsule" in Beijing, and in factory workshops, with plans to scale deployment in 2026.5,6 Singapore-based Sharpa showcased a humanoid playing fully autonomous, fluid ping pong against a human competitor.

I also met white-label humanoid manufacturers. I spoke to one in particular, Lingyi iTech, which told me it is the integrator behind many flagship humanoid initiatives. They can handle everything from specific components of the humanoid supply chain to the production of entire models for their clients.

In a sign that humanoids are attracting serious corporate attention beyond automakers like Tesla (TSLA) and XPeng (XPEV), LG unveiled its entry into the space with CLOiD - a laundry-folding humanoid robot (whose name is similar to a certain humanoid ETF ticker). More importantly, LG’s massive booth featured prominent displays highlighting its humanoid actuator manufacturing capabilities - another key theme featured throughout the conference.

Having been immersed in the humanoid robotics world since the launch of KOID last June, much of this felt both incredible - and increasingly expected. From what I saw, robot movement is becoming more fluid. Designs are wildly varied, reminiscent of the early smartphone era before convergence around a dominant form factor. Based on my observations, the pace of progress is moving quickly.

What truly stopped me in my tracks, however, were the advances in the brains of these robots.

Building the Robot Brain

One company in particular stood out: IntBot, based in San Jose, California. At CES, I spent time with their CTO, Dr. Sharon Yang, who explained how IntBot partnered with AgiBot to deploy humanoids powered by agentic AI for real-world service roles. Their first customers include hotels installing autonomous concierge robots at the Tulsa Marriott, the Nap York pod hotel in New York City, and the Otonomus Hotel in Las Vegas.

Dr. Yang was clear about where the frontier has shifted. The hardest problem in humanoid robotics is no longer mechanical movement - it is instant human understanding. Recognizing intent. Tracking gestures like a handshake. Adapting personality. Responding in real time without cloud latency.

I experienced this firsthand at the IntBot demo. The robot didn’t just speak fluently - it spoke naturally. When I asked whether it was familiar with our KOID ETF, it immediately responded yes, cited several holdings, and then began pitching why IntBot itself belonged in the portfolio. It wasn’t scripted. It reasoned. It was an awesome salesperson!

Dr. Yang mentioned the system could speak more than 50 languages fluently, so we tested it. Mandarin - perfect. Spanish - also perfect. Then someone challenged it in Farsi. To everyone’s surprise, its Farsi was flawless. The takeaway was evident: the next leap in humanoids isn’t motion - it’s interaction. In many respects, I think IntBot has already surpassed human capability in this domain.

Training Data

Movement is improving. Intelligence is advancing. But beneath both lies a quieter, less visible dependency: training data.

That became clear during a conversation at the PaXini booth.

PaXini was drawing steady attention for its humanoid hands and tactile sensing systems. The hardware demonstrations were polished and well attended. Inside the booth, however, a more understated process was underway. A real human technician sat slightly off to the side, wearing sensor-equipped gloves, repeatedly picking up and placing everyday objects.

I spoke with Jeremy Li, who explained that this activity was not a secondary demonstration, but a core part of how humanoid robots are trained.

While mechanical systems and AI models have advanced rapidly, generalized humanoid capability still depends on large volumes of high-quality real-world interaction data. Programming alone is insufficient. Robots must learn how humans grasp objects, apply pressure, adjust mid-motion, and recover from errors across varied environments.

PaXini’s approach relies on human-in-the-loop data collection. Operators wear gloves, vision systems, and motion-capture equipment inside standardized data-collection rooms. According to Jeremy, these sessions capture fine-grained information - grip force, pressure, orientation, and motion timing - paired with synchronized visual data. The resulting datasets are used to train embodied AI models governing how robots manipulate objects autonomously and safely.

Jeremy described PaXini’s dedicated data-collection facility in China, spanning approximately 12,000 square meters and employing roughly 500 staff focused exclusively on generating this training data.

According to Jeremy, for humanoids to move beyond controlled demonstrations and into service, hospitality, healthcare, and manufacturing environments, they must generalize across tasks and settings. He believes that generalization depends less on incremental software changes and more on exposure to real human behavior at scale.

Don’t forget the actuators!

My next “aha” moment came from a very different conversation.

I spoke with Houston Yu from ZeroErr, a humanoid actuator manufacturer based in Shenzhen with operations across Asia and the U.S. If IntBot represented the rapid evolution of humanoid intelligence, Houston explained where the economic reality of humanoids truly resides: actuators.

Houston walked me through ZeroErr’s integrated rotary actuator modules - compact systems that combine motors, drivers, dual encoders, harmonic gearboxes, brakes, and control electronics into a single unit. This level of integration is essential. Humanoid robots require precise, zero-backlash motion and real-time feedback at every joint to operate safely around humans.

Every joint in a humanoid requires an actuator. A single human arm has seven degrees of freedom - meaning seven actuators per arm. When I pointed to a nearby EngineAI humanoid and asked how many actuators it might contain, Houston estimated roughly 43 across the full body.

Actuators, he explained, can account for as much as 70% of a humanoid robot’s total production cost. That makes them not just a technical bottleneck, but the primary economic driver of humanoid scalability. It also explains why so much innovation is focused on integration - reducing part counts, improving reliability, and driving down system-level costs.

Conclusion: From CES Signal to Portfolio Exposure

CES 2026 made one thing clear: humanoid robotics has moved beyond isolated demonstrations and into early commercial reality. Integrators are delivering full systems. Intelligence is becoming conversational and adaptive. Training data is being industrialized. And the cost structure - particularly around actuators - is coming into focus.

This progress is no longer dependent on a single breakthrough. It reflects multiple layers advancing together. When movement, intelligence, data, and economics begin to align, adoption can follow. CES has signaled that pattern before, and I believe humanoids are following suit.

For investors, the opportunity is compelling but complex. The humanoid ecosystem is global, supply-chain driven, and multidisciplinary. Companies are distributed across regions, with China central to integration and manufacturing, the U.S. driving AI and software, and Europe and Asia contributing critical components. Isolating individual winners risks missing the broader system enabling scale.

That is why we built the KraneShares Global Humanoid and Embodied Intelligence ETF (KOID).

KOID provides differentiated exposure across the humanoid value chain - integrators bringing robots to market, intelligence platforms enabling interaction, data infrastructure training models, and component manufacturers shaping cost and reliability. CES reinforced that this framework reflects how the industry is already organizing itself.

Humanoid robots are moving into hotels, factories, classrooms, and retail environments. I believe that the question for investors is no longer if they matter, but how to potentially gain exposure as adoption expands.

This material contains the author's opinions. It is provided for informational purposes only and should not be regarded as investment advice or a recommendation of specific securities. Holdings are subject to change. Securities mentioned do not make up the entire portfolio and, in the aggregate, may represent a small percentage of the fund. A balanced evaluation of both risks and potential rewards is essential when considering any investment. This material does not constitute a forecast or guarantee of future performance.

Citations:

  1. Data from "The global market for humanoid robots could reach $38 billion by 2035," Goldman Sachs, retrieved 1/20/2026.
  2. Data from "A $5 Trillion Global Market," Morgan Stanley, retrieved 1/20/2026.
  3. Data from "CES 2026: The Future is Here," Consumer Technology Association, as of 1/9/2026.
  4. Data from "Longest journey walked by a humanoid robot," Guinness World Records, retrieved 1/20/2026.
  5. Data from "Galaxy General Robotics wins the World Humanoid Robot Games, leading the pack with fully autonomous operation and showcasing its impressive landing capabilities," Weixin, as of 8/17/2025.
  6. Data from "Galbot’s autonomous retail clerk expands into real-world store deployments," Galbot Inc, as of 10/3/2025.

Definitions:

Actuators: The mechanical components (essentially motors) that enable robots to move their joints.

Integrated rotary actuator modules: Compact motorized units that combine multiple motion-control parts (motor, sensors, brakes, gears) for precise robotic movements.

Human-in-the-loop data collection: A process where people perform tasks that generate real-world data to train AI models - used here to teach robots how humans move and interact physically.

Embodied intelligence: AI embedded in a physical machine (like a robot) that allows it to perceive and act in the real world.

Agentic AI: An AI system capable of autonomous decision-making and goal-directed behavior with minimal human supervision.

Cloud latency: The delay between a request sent to the cloud (remote servers) and the response received - important for real-time robotic actions.

Degrees of freedom: The number of independent ways a joint or mechanism (like a robot arm) can move.

Zero-backlash motion: Precise mechanical movement without looseness or delay - important for robots working safely with humans.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: URA: Warning Signals
data.description:
The Global X Uranium ETF (URA) exceeded its October '25 peak this week after a strong +6.85% one-day gain. It's risen +44% since my last article in November, which suggested to "Buy The 50% Retracement." This article looks at what drove the move and why commodity markets are flashing a warning signal.

URA Basics

URA is managed by Global X and is based on the Solactive Global Uranium & Nuclear Components Total Return Index. It provides broad exposure to companies involved in uranium mining and nuclear power generation. Stocks are selected by a committee and weighted according to rules.

A Committee composed of staff from Solactive is responsible for decisions regarding the composition of the Index as well as any amendments to the rules (in this document referred to as the “Committee” or the “Index Committee”). The future composition of the Index is determined by the Committee on the Selection Days.

There is international exposure, and Canada is the top-weighted country.

This is primarily due to Cameco's heavy weighting, which is three times larger than second-place Oklo (OKLO).

I don't particularly like the composition, with one company dominating and the second position taken by a company without any revenue (but plenty of promise). I actually prefer the Sprott Uranium Miners ETF (URNM), which looks better balanced and has Cameco at a 20% weighting and second-place Uranium Energy Corp. (UEC) at 13%. Most importantly, it has outperformed over the longer term.

But this article is about URA, and it's a decent fund for broad exposure and has grown in popularity in the last two years.

This is driven by the U-turn in global sentiment over uranium and nuclear energy. Following Fukushima, nuclear was frowned on. It is now seen as essential to global energy needs, and the huge electricity demands of AI have accelerated this sentiment.

A Significant Rally

How's this for volatility? URA rallied 210% from the April '25 low to the October high, retraced exactly 50% of that rally, and has now rallied another 54% to a new high.

These moves are part of the larger trend driven by increasing demands for nuclear and for electricity. This trend is likely to persist for several more years as existing companies expand, and newer companies such as OKLO ramp up operations.

Smaller moves like the drops in 2025 tend to unfold when moves become overextended. Earnings season in October may have contributed to the 50% retracement, as it reminded investors that this rally is not yet backed up by earnings.

The macro trend is helped on by a steady trickle of good news, especially in areas like investment and deregulation. This week's move in URA was partly driven by this news story:

The DoE said it is seeking input from U.S. states on any interest they have in hosting sites for developing the nuclear fuel lifecycle, which includes storing nuclear waste and spent fuel reprocessing.

The sites, called Nuclear Lifecycle Innovation Campuses, could offer federal support for deployment of advanced nuclear reactors and co-located data centers, which also could enrich uranium, the agency said.

This is just one of many U.S. initiatives, not only in the nuclear sector but in the wider utilities sector too. There's no doubt the backdrop is positive, but the question for URA investors is whether the rally is getting ahead of itself again?

It's a difficult question to answer. Valuations are not much help as URA's holdings are diverse and contain companies without positive earnings. According to the Global X site, the P/E ratio in 2025 was an elevated 64, which is expected to drop to 37.5 in 2026. In any case, it will still be elevated, and the rally is pricing in a lot of good news.

Q4 earnings season may be another painful reminder that earnings growth in this sector is slow and, when viewed through the lens of a 200-300% rally, is hard to justify.

While the underlying trend is driven by fundamentals, sentiment has a large say in the smaller moves and may be flashing a warning signal.

The Commodity Boom

Commodity markets have reached extreme levels of volatility. As I am writing this, copper is +7%, gold is +2.5%, and it has gained nearly 30% in January. Earlier this week, silver rose 14% and dropped 14% in one session.

URA is also volatile and rose +6.85% on Wednesday.

There are several reasons for this volatility. The US dollar (DXY) is dropping fast, and there is a rush to own assets. My concern is that it seems the FOMO is getting panicky. The rally in precious metals is accelerating, and the trend has gone parabolic, which is unsustainable.

I'm not calling a top, but I do think buying commodities—or URA - under this backdrop is speculative and risky.

Long-term, there are strong tailwinds, so any drop shouldn't be a huge concern, but in the medium term, I think this rally may struggle to sustain a break over the October peak, and earnings season may lead to a drop. I have closed some of the positions I picked up near $40 and would look at buying again near $50.

Conclusions

URA has exceeded the October peak as the strong macro trend and positive news flow continue. This trend should have further to go, but the current volatility is flashing shorter-term warning signals, and earnings season disappointment could weigh. I'm not recommending to sell, but I would think hard about buying at these levels.

data.title: First Financial Bancorp.: Continued Momentum
data.description:
We resume our Q4 earnings season regional bank coverage with a dividend growth name in First Financial Bancorp. (FFBC). Our review in totality of the space can help us determine the health of the U.S. economy from the ground up. Thus far this earnings season, the regional banks have shown a lot of strength. Overall, we have seen loan and deposit growth, margin strength, and growing earnings. We have noted some modest asset quality softening in some select banks, but nothing to worry about. Here we review the just-reported Q4 earnings.

First Financial Bancorp. Q4 headline earnings and margins

First Financial reported a 12.1% increase in top-line revenues to $251.3 million from the previous year's Q4, and this surpassed consensus estimates by $2.4 million. Make no mistake, net interest income remains strong here with this bank as well. In fact, like many other regionals we have covered, margins remain strong. However, it dipped slightly from the sequential Q3, down 4 basis points, to 3.98% on a fully tax-equivalent basis. Even though it is not over 4% right now, this is still among the higher-end margins for all regional banks we cover. This was driven by a 15-point decrease in the cost of funds, while yields on assets were down 19 basis points. Both are down due to lower interest rates.

The bank's net interest income for the quarter hit $174.0 million, up from $154.4 million last year. Factoring in well-managed expenses, we saw an earnings beat of $0.13 per share, with net income hitting $0.80 per share. How about loans and deposits?

Loan and deposit growth

Now, the bank did do an acquisition of Westfield, which helped bump the loan and deposit book some. With every bank we have covered here in the Q4 earnings season, we look at loan and deposit trends. First Financial saw growth in loans compared to the sequential quarter. Total loans were $13.4 billion, up from $11.71 billion.

As for deposits, they increased to $16.4 billion, versus $14.4 billion in Q3. While the large bump was influenced by the acquisitor, there had been modest organic growth in 2025 as well.

Return metrics and asset quality

Now, a merger comes with added costs and a transition period that can last a few quarters, so we did see some variance in the return metrics and asset quality that are worth noting. The return on average assets slipped to 1.22% from 1.54% in Q3. The return on average equity also fell to 16.27% from 19.11% in Q3. Still, both of these metrics are respectable on an absolute basis. Loan loss provisions were down slightly, from $9.71 million to $9.68 million. The efficiency ratio was still a strong 56.5% as adjusted.

Nonperforming assets were up 7 basis points from 0.41% of all assets in Q3 to 0.48% of all assets. This is the highest they have been in many quarters. These totaled $102 million, jumping from $76 million in the sequential quarter, driven by the merger, but the percentage metric is more important to lean on. Something to watch in 2026. The allowance for credit losses held firm at 1.39% of all loans, a single basis point tick up from 1.38%. Net charge-offs were 0.27% of all loans, which is in the middle of a range for the last year.

We note that tangible book value per share actually fell to $15.74, a 2.8% dip from Q3. It is also up from $14.15 a year ago. This is strong. It is worth noting that tangible book value per share has increased by over 30% in the last three years. Shares are trading at a premium, however, at $28 per share. However, given the performance and the moderately high yield and growing dividend, we think a premium is justified.

Final thoughts

The FFBC dividend is up to $0.25 per quarter per share, and the yield is a respectable 3.7%. We like that it grows. The Westfield acquisition will result in a couple of transitional quarters until return metrics and asset quality settle out. However, we think that the asset quality and return metrics as reported are still reflective of a quality bank. Margins remain strong, and earnings are growing. We think you should stick with shares if you own them.

data.title: Wall Street Lunch: Software Stocks Slammed
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Software stocks slide sharply as Microsoft, ServiceNow stoke fears. (0:15) Caterpillar beats earnings as AI data-center demand lifts sales. (1:57) Barry Diller wants CNN. (2:59)

This is an abridged transcript of the podcast:

Our top story so far, software struggles are getting hardcore.

The sector is selling off sharply, taking tech and the broader market down with it. The iShares Expanded Tech-Software ETF (IGV) is down more than 5% — a drop we haven’t seen since tariff Liberation Day.

That’s dragged down the Nasdaq (COMP.IND), while bitcoin (BTC-USD), gold (GLD) and silver (SLV) also saw simultaneous selling. All those asset classes are now off their lows.

At the coalface of the software slump is Microsoft (MSFT), which you heard on Wall Street Breakfast was sinking despite solid, if unspectacular, quarterly results. Selling has ramped up through the session, with the stock off about 12% — on track for the seventh-worst decline in its history.

Evercore analyst Kirk Materne says the debate is “no longer about demand; it is about capacity timing (and perhaps allocation).”

“While Azure growth at these levels remains impressive and continues to suggest market share gains, capex rose 66% year over year, and investors are increasingly looking for clearer evidence that this elevated investment is translating into incremental Azure acceleration,” he added.

The real trigger, though, may have been ServiceNow (NOW), down more than 10% despite strong results.

Analysts noted that 21.5% subscription revenue growth “did not quite represent acceleration,” but were still broadly positive. Citizens reiterated its Market Outperform rating, pointing to revenue growth, free cash flow margin and a strong leadership team.

Even so, the stock has now shed about 50% of its value over the past year.

Also heavily in the red are Atlassian (TEAM), Salesforce (CRM), Workday (WDAY) and DataDog (DOG).

Among other active stocks, Caterpillar (CAT) continues to benefit from AI — specifically data center demand — with Q4 results beating expectations.

CAT said fourth-quarter sales rose to $19.1B, above the $17.76B consensus. Adjusted EPS came in at $5.16, easily topping the $4.71 expected.

Power and energy sales climbed 23% to $9.4B. Within that segment, power generation revenue jumped 44% to $3.24B, driven by higher sales of large reciprocating engines that the company said were primarily tied to data-center applications, as the AI buildout keeps echoing through demand.

Altria (MO) is under pressure after slightly missing profit estimates in Q4. The tobacco giant said it’s targeting mid-single-digit annual dividend-per-share growth through 2028. Altria has boosted its dividend 60 times in the last 56 years.

And Joby Aviation (JOBY) is sinking after pricing an offering. The electric vertical take-off-and-landing company will issue $600M of 0.75% convertible senior notes due 2032 and sell about 52.9M shares of common stock at $11.35 per share.

And in other news of note, Barry Diller has expressed interest in buying CNN and approached Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) about a deal last year.

The Wall Street Journal reports that Diller inquired about CNN before Warner announced its plan to split into two distinct businesses — and before WBD formally put itself up for sale.

Sources close to Warner told the Journal that no serious action was taken on Diller’s approach, and it wasn’t brought to the board. But Diller is still said to be interested in CNN and is eyeing a deal in a personal capacity.

data.title: AgomAb Therapeutics NV Readies US IPO On Early Promising Results
data.description:
A Quick Take On AgomAb

AgomAb Therapeutics NV (AGMB) has filed to raise $200 million in development capital in an IPO of its American Depositary Shares representing underlying common shares, according to SEC F-1/A registration information.

The company is a clinical-stage biopharma advancing treatment candidates for chronic fibrotic conditions.

It has achieved positive safety results and early promising signals on exploratory clinical endpoints.

Given these early results, its strong institutional life science VC, and strategic big pharma investor backing, the IPO will likely see significant institutional investor demand.

For patient life science investors, my outlook on the AGMB IPO is a Buy at up to $16.00 per share.

AgomAb Overview and Development Efforts

Antwerpen, Belgium-based AgomAb Therapeutics NV is developing chronic fibrosis treatments for severe conditions "through inhibition of one of the key signaling pathways involved in fibrosis, the transforming growth factor β, or TGFβ, pathway."

Management is headed by Chief Executive Officer and Executive Director, Mr. Tim Knotnerus, who has been with the firm since February 2019 and has held senior positions at AM-Pharma B.V. and was a Senior Associate at Aescap Venture, a European venture capital fund investing in European medical companies.

The company's lead treatment candidate, AGMB-129 (Ontunisertib), is in Phase 2a trials for the treatment of fibrostenosing Crohn's Disease.

Management expects to have full open-label extension data in the second half of 2026.

AGMB is in Phase 1 trials for AGMB-447 for the treatment of idiopathic (unknown origin) pulmonary fibrosis.

The status of the company’s current development pipeline is shown below:

AgomAb has recorded a fair market value investment of $365 million through September 30, 2025, from investors, including LSP, Fidelity Management & Research, Pontifax, Sanofi Foreign Participations (SNY), Redmile Biopharma Investments, Cormorant Asset Management, Pfizer (PFE), and Canaan.

AgomAb’s Markets & Competitors

Per a 2025 market research report by Data Bridge Market Research, the global market for fibrotic disease treatments was an estimated $5.8 billion in 2024 and is expected to exceed $10.3 billion by 2032.

This represents a forecast CAGR of 7.6% from 2025 to 2032.

The main reasons for this expected growth are a growing prevalence of fibrotic diseases due to aging populations, lifestyle alterations, and chronic contributing conditions such as hypertension and diabetes.

Also, the chart below shows the historical and projected future growth path of the fibrotic disease treatment market through 2030 by region:

Major competitive companies that have developed or are developing similar treatments include the following firms:

  • Merck (MRK)

  • Sanofi

  • Roche Holding (RHHBY)

  • Boehringer Ingelheim International GmbH

  • Bristol Myers Squibb (BMY)

  • Amgen (AMGN)

  • Numerous others

The fibrotic condition treatment market has typically focused on managing symptoms or inflammation.

AgomAb’s treatment approach is to focus on restoring tissue homeostasis by reprogramming or inhibiting activated fibroblasts back to an inactive state.

AGMB’s pipeline is designed to be organ-agnostic, with potential applicability in GI, liver, and other fibrotic indications.

AgomAb Financial Results

AgomAb’s recent financial results are typical of a development-stage biopharma firm in that they feature high R&D and G&A costs associated with their pipeline advancement efforts and no revenue.

According to its current IPO filing, the table below shows the company's financial results for the periods indicated:

As of September 30, 2025, the company had $151.6 million in cash and $11.1 million in total liabilities.

AgomAb IPO Details

AgomAb will sell 12.5 million American Depositary Shares representing underlying common shares at a proposed midpoint price of $16.00 per share for gross proceeds of $200 million.

The company didn’t disclose any proposed institutional purchasers of stock in the IPO, whether from existing or new investors.

Shareholder, company, and executive shareholder sale lock-ups will be for the normal & customary 180 days after the effective date of the IPO prospectus.

Management is asking IPO investors to value the company at an enterprise value of approximately $497 million, within the typical range of a high-quality biotech firm at IPO, so the valuation appears reasonable.

AgomAb said it intends to use the IPO net proceeds as described below:

to advance clinical development of ontunisertib, including for the planned global Phase 2b trial in FSCD patients;

to advance clinical development of AGMB-447, including for the planned Phase 2 trial in IPF patients; and

the remainder to fund advancement of preclinical candidates, general and administrative expenses, working capital, one milestone payment pursuant to our acquisition agreement relating to Origo Biopharma, S.L. and other general corporate purposes.

(Source: SEC)

Leadership’s online company roadshow presentation is not available to the public.

Regarding legal proceedings against the firm or its directors or employees, management said the company is not presently a party to any legal proceedings of a material amount.

The listed bookrunners of the IPO are J.P. Morgan, Morgan Stanley, Leerink Partners, and Van Lanschot Kempen.

My Thoughts About AgomAb’s IPO

AGMB is seeking US public market capital to fund advancing its programs for serious, chronic fibrotic treatments.

The firm’s lead candidate, AGMB-129 (Ontunisertib), is in Phase 2a trials for the treatment of fibrostenosing Crohn's Disease.

AgomAb said it plans to have full open-label extension data in the second half of 2026.

For its lead program, its Phase 2a trial results reached its primary endpoint for safety and tolerability and “also observed positive signals on several exploratory clinical endpoints.”

The market opportunity for fibrotic condition treatments is large and expected to grow at a moderate rate of growth, but the company faces significant competition from major market participants.

AGMB hasn’t disclosed any major pharma firm collaboration agreements or relationships.

However, the company’s investor syndicate includes strategic investors Sanofi and Pfizer, along with several well-known institutional life science venture capital firms, and is well-capitalized.

It is uncommon for a biopharma to have two strategic investors and is noteworthy.

Other risks for investors include its ‘foreign private issuer’ and ‘emerging growth company’ status, which lets management disclose substantially less information to investors, at its option.

Many such company stocks have performed poorly post-IPO, although they have not typically been in the life science industry.

Given the company's progress to date, including promising early efficacy results and its strong institutional life science venture capital and strategic investor backing, the IPO will likely be in strong demand from institutional investors.

For life science investors with a patient hold time frame, my outlook on the AGMB IPO is a Buy at up to $16.00 per share.

Expected IPO Pricing Date: February 5, 2026

data.title: FAX: Fixed-Income Fund With Primary Exposure To Asia-Pacific Region
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman, co-produced by Stanford Chemist

The last time we gave the abrdn Asia-Pacific Income Fund (FAX) a look, it was touching on the potential merger of abrdn Global Income Fund Inc (FCO) into FAX. This would make FAX slightly larger in terms of total managed assets than it is today. However, FCO is quite a small fund at around $60 million AUM compared to FAX, standing at over $1.1 billion. With that, it would be relatively immaterial to FAX. It would easily liquidate the current portfolio and absorb it into FAX's own underlying strategy.

In that piece, I highlighted mostly FCO and how we viewed FCO as a 'Sell' because of its enormous premium. When that merger was announced, it was a very rapid decline for FCO. This was because FAX did not trade at even close to the same valuation that FCO had at the time.

So FCO sank rapidly to meet the same level that FAX was trading at. Since that announcement, FAX continues to trade at a wider discount, but not at a level that makes it attractive enough to consider adding aggressively, in my opinion. On the other hand, the fund's rather high distribution rate is likely enticing to some investors—the problem on that front is that the coverage of the distribution is rather weak in terms of income. Therefore, the fund will rely on requiring capital gains to fund its full distribution, or it would risk seeing NAV erode.

FAX Basics

  • 1-Year Z-score: 0.07
  • Discount/Premium: -5.66%
  • Distribution Yield: 12.77%
  • Expense Ratio: 1.41%
  • Leverage: 38.58%
  • Managed Assets: $1.104 billion
  • Structure: Perpetual

FAX's investment objective is to "seek current income". In an effort to meet that investment objective, the fund will:

normally invest at least 80% of its total assets, plus the amount of any borrowings for investment purposes, in "Asia-Pacific debt securities,” which include: (1) debt securities of Asia-Pacific Country issuers, including securities issued by Asia-Pacific Country governmental entities, as well as by banks, companies and other entities which are located in Asia-Pacific Countries, whether or not denominated in an Asia-Pacific Country currency; (2) debt securities of other issuers, denominated in, or linked to, the currency of an Asia-Pacific Country, including securities issued by supranational issuers, such as The World Bank and derivative debt securities that replicate, or substitute for, the currency of an Asia-Pacific Country; (3) debt securities issued by entities which, although not located in an Asia-Pacific Country, derive at least 50% of their revenues from Asia-Pacific Countries or have at least 50% of their assets located in Asia-Pacific Countries; and (4) debt securities issued by a wholly-owned subsidiary of an entity located in an Asia-Pacific Country, provided that the debt securities are guaranteed by the parent entity located in the Asia-Pacific Country (the “80% Policy”). With reference to items (3) and (4) above, Asia-Pacific debt securities may be denominated in an Asia-Pacific Country currency or U.S. dollars. “Asia-Pacific Countries” (each, an “Asia-Pacific Country”) means countries included in “Asia” and “Oceania” in the United Nations (“UN”) geographic regions used by the UN Statistics Division.

When including the leverage expenses, which this fund is fairly heavily leveraged, the total expense ratio comes in at 4.22%. That was actually up substantially from last year's 3.31%.

They operate with a credit facility, and that comes with a floating rate-based debt. The latest annual report showed it coming in at 5.87% on average for fiscal 2025. As the Fed eases its target rate, that will help to also ease the borrowing costs for FAX. That's why it is still interesting that despite the 75 basis points of cuts at the end of 2024, expenses still rose.

They also have some senior secured notes and preferred shares that they employ for leverage, and these come with attractive fixed rates. Below are the senior notes and the interest rates associated with those. The Series B Mandatory Redeemable Preferred Shares come in with an annual dividend of 5.391%.

That would have helped to keep some of their borrowing costs from seeing an increase. Of course, it also means now that a large portion of their leverage also won't benefit from reduced rates.

FAX Acquiring FCO Isn't Guaranteed

Interesting to note on the potential FCO merger into FAX is that they've also included in the latest filing that there is also a plan of liquidation potentially that FCO investors can also vote on. In that case, the merger with FAX would not end up going through, and FCO would simply liquidate at NAV.

1. Agreement and Plan of Reorganization. To approve an Agreement and Plan of Reorganization providing for the transfer of all of the assets of the Acquired Fund to abrdn Asia-Pacific Income Fund, Inc. (the “Acquiring Fund”) in exchange solely for newly issued common stock of the Acquiring Fund (although cash may be distributed in lieu of fractional shares) and the assumption by the Acquiring Fund of all or substantially all liabilities of the Acquired Fund and the distribution of common stock of the Acquiring Fund to the stockholders of the Acquired Fund and complete liquidation of the Acquired Fund (the “Reorganization”). The Acquiring Fund as it would exist after the Reorganization is referred to as the “Combined Fund.”

2. Plan of Liquidation. To approve a plan of liquidation and dissolution (the “Liquidation Plan”) of the Acquired Fund (the “Liquidation”). The Liquidation will only occur if the Liquidation Plan is approved by stockholders of the Acquired Fund and the Reorganization is not approved by stockholders of the Acquired Fund or is not consummated for any other reason. If only the Reorganization Agreement is approved, or if both the Reorganization Agreement and the Liquidation Plan are approved, the Reorganization will occur.

The special meeting isn't being held until March 12, 2026, so investors have quite a bit of time to decide. However, at the present time, actually voting for liquidation for FCO investors makes the most sense. This is because while they'd see immediate downside for accepting the current NAV, they actually could see even further downside in accepting new shares of FAX.

For FAX, trading at a wide discount has been quite normal, and once that merger was announced, they actually dropped even further. That is, even though the merger actually has a limited impact on FAX overall. As we noted at the open, it would add roughly $60 million in AUM for a fund that is already over $1.1 billion.

Distribution - Enticing Payout, Watching Coverage

FAX delivers a solid distribution rate to investors, coming in at 12.77%, and the NAV rate comes in at 12.05%.

That's significantly lower than FCO's distribution rate, but FCO's was incredibly unsustainable. FCO's distribution rate is also about to be either zero or in line with FAX's payout.

With that said, while it would be a relatively more moderate rate compared to its sister fund that it may acquire, it is hardly looking like it is in great shape in terms of coverage. At least, when looking at net investment income, the NII coverage came in at less than 36%.

In other words, ~64% of the payout will need to be funded via capital gains and appreciation in the underlying portfolio and investment strategy. Impacting the coverage was the result of both higher operating expenses for the fund and lower total investment income. In fiscal 2024, TII came in at $65.3 million, and that eased to $58.5 million. Total expenses rose to $29.338 million from the $24.57 million. So that really squeezed NII on both sides of the equation.

For tax purposes, they actually listed that fiscal 2025's distribution was almost entirely return of capital. This would likely be the result of significant capital loss carryforwards that were utilized. As of the end of their fiscal year, they were still sitting on around $193 million.

However, it is also the result of overpaying the distribution, after accounting for NII and the realized/unrealized gains/losses, FAX saw a decline in its NAV year-over-year.

FAX's Portfolio

As the fund's name would imply, and along with the fund's investment objective, they are heavily concentrated in Asia-Pacific fixed-income investments. In particular, the fund is heavily tilted toward holding government debt of this geographic region. The largest sector allocation is neraly 50% allocated to government debt.

Some of this debt is paying quite a high rate as well; in particular, they are holding some of Mexico's debt at an 8.5% rate. Which is actually interesting on its own because Mexico is not an Asia-Pacific nation, nor is South Africa. However, keep in mind that the policy is that they "normally invest at least 80% of their total assets". That leaves them some room to incorporate some other geographic regions within their portfolio and still be well within their investment policy.

With that being said, with some of this higher-rated debt comes relatively lower-rated credit quality within the portfolio. The fund has the highest allocation to investment-grade securities at BBB or above, coming in at 73.2%. However, BBB is only the first rung into investment-grade quality, highlighting the relatively greater risks of some of these nations despite being "government" debt.

Finally, I would highlight that given the geographic allocation of the fund, there is some currency exposure here outside of USD alone. Though USD is the largest allocation of the fund.

To help position the fund with hedges, the management team can utilize some more complex strategies than simply investing directly into underlying debt investments. That includes future contracts on U.S. Treasuries but elsewhere around the globe. Further, they also employ forward foreign currency contracts. That also helps to position them in various ways that the management team feels are appropriate at a given time.

Conclusion

FAX provides a fixed-income portfolio with a heavy concentration in Asia-Pacific countries, particularly the government offerings, as it comprises the largest allocation of the portfolio by far. On its own, FAX seems like a fairly solid fixed-income offering providing relatively unique exposure. The discount is fairly appealing here, but not particularly at a massive bargain. The distribution is quite high, which is appealing, but that will also require some significant capital gains to fully cover. So the long-term sustainability is something I would question. However, since it isn't trading at a substantial premium, that isn't a particularly significant overhanging risk if they cut their payout.

Not like we had seen with something like FCO—which FAX is set to potentially acquire this sister fund. For FAX, this would not alter the fund too materially, given the significant size difference. It also isn't necessarily guaranteed to occur, either, as investors in FCO have the choice to potentially liquidate their holdings.

At present, liquidating their entire holding at NAV would make more sense because while it is currently trading at a slight premium, FAX is at a larger discount. Essentially, investors could liquidate at NAV and then buy FAX at an even better price instead of accepting the new shares and immediately accepting those shares that are trading below NAV. With that said, we have a couple of months until the special meeting, so keeping a close eye on the discount/premium levels will be important.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Best Staples Stocks As Consumer Confidence Hits Multi-Year Low
data.description:
Consumer Staples Shift from Laggard to Leader in 2026

The consumer staples sector is off to a strong start in 2026, on pace for its best January performance in over a decade. This momentum is driven by renewed investor appetite for value and defensive stocks after years of tech’s growth dominance. After lagging broad markets in 2025 as AI-fueled gains captured headlines, staples have gained attention, as inflation moderates, rate cut expectations firm, and fiscal stimulus potentially supports consumer spending on essentials. Yet consumer confidence has dipped to its lowest level in over a decade. While I’ve recently highlighted select AI stocks that can outperform in 2026, the consumer staples sector (XLP) is significantly outperforming technology (XLK) year-to-date:

With grocery price growth still elevated and discount retail gaining share, staples can continue to grow even as consumer confidence remains challenged. Policy tailwinds like anticipated tax cuts could lift disposable income, helping sustain demand for staples in 2026, even as consumers remain cost-conscious. Investors looking for defensive exposure amid market volatility can consider opportunities not just in large cap staple giants, but in niche and value-oriented names poised to benefit from these macro trends.

How I Chose the Best Consumer Staples Stocks

While investors can easily create custom screens with the Seeking Alpha Stock Screener, there are 30 pre-screened categories of stocks, including one for the top consumer staples stocks, which I used to select the ones in this article. The stocks are automatically sorted with Quant Strong Buys at the top, and I chose four that possess Quant Factors that indicate potential for continued growth in 2026.

1. Village Farms International, Inc. (VFF)

  • Market Capitalization: $392.76M

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 01/29/2026): 1 out of 179

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 01/29/2026): 1 out of 11

  • Sector: Consumer Staples

  • Industry: Agricultural Products & Services

Village Farms International operates vertically integrated greenhouse agriculture, producing fresh produce for U.S. and Canadian retailers and branching into high-growth wellness segments, including CBD products and cannabis. Its unique blend of essential products and exposure to emerging consumer health trends has helped drive 350%+ gains for VFF over the past year. Like many of the growth stocks I analyze, VFF sells at a premium P/E (non-GAAP) relative to peers, but its forward growth provides support for the higher price and potential for more room to run. Supporting VFF’s ‘A+’ Growth grade is its forward EBITDA growth metric of nearly 95%, which is more than 20 times the sector median of 4.35%

Profit margins are below sector median, but they are moving in the right direction, as evidenced by CEO Mike DeGiglio stating that "our third quarter was another one of many records for Village Farms" and emphasizing a 21% year-over-year increase in consolidated net sales and net income from continuing operations of $10.8 million, or $0.09 per share. As a small-cap in the consumer staples sector, which is skewed by mega-caps like Procter & Gamble Company (PG) and Walmart Inc. (WMT), profitability metrics typically screen below medians. A highlight for VFF is its Levered FCF Margin of 11.04%, which is more than double the sector median, signaling that the company is generating higher free cash flow after interest and debt relative to its revenue base.

Village Farms’ focus on essential food products and health-oriented goods positions it well within an inflation-sensitive macro backdrop as consumers focus on value and quality. As investors seek consumer staples with differentiated growth, VFF’s strategic mix of core agriculture and health wellness segments keeps it compelling. This creates a good starting point and transition into a smaller, direct-to-consumer supplements play.

2. Nature's Sunshine Products, Inc. (NATR)

  • Market Capitalization: $421.64M

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 01/29/2026): 3 out of 179

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 01/29/2026): 1 out of 31

  • Sector: Consumer Staples

  • Industry: Personal Care Products

Nature’s Sunshine Products is a global wellness company producing and distributing a range of nutritional supplements and personal care products through a network of independent distributors. The company has delivered outsized returns relative to many sector peers, supported by growing autoship subscriptions and international diversification. Recent earnings beat expectations, reflecting resilient demand for health and immune-support products and operational improvements.

NATR’s Growth Grade is a ‘B-’ and it’s improving as it was a ‘C+’ three months ago and a ‘C’ six months ago. Reflected in the growth metrics, NATR’s 8.21% forward EBITDA growth shows tremendous improvement from its YoY measure, and is nearly 90% higher than sector peers. Looking ahead, NATR can continue to grow if recent trends continue as well. As its CFO and Treasurer Shane Jones pointed out at last quarter’s earnings call, “we continue to see improved momentum as digital accelerates, while maintaining our core business of specialty retailers, practitioners, affiliates and business builders.” Jones highlighted particular strength in North American digital business year-over-year growth, which accelerated to 52% in Q3. If that strength carries into 2026, NATR can continue its 6-month outperformance vs peers.

In the current macro environment, where consumers remain cautious but prioritize health and value, NATR’s product breadth and distributor network offer a solid platform for sustained growth. These strengths segue into beverage giants like Ambev, where brand power within staples also serves as a buffer in uncertain times.

3. Ambev S.A. (ABEV)

  • Market Capitalization: $44.85B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 01/29/2026): 2 out of 179

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 01/29/2026): 1 out of 7

  • Sector: Consumer Staples

  • Industry: Brewers

Ambev is one of Latin America’s largest beverage companies, boasting an extensive portfolio of consumer favorites across beer, such as Budweiser and Stella Artois, and non-alcoholic drinks, including Pepsi and Gatorade. Its scale, cost-efficient operations, and strong brand positioning have enabled it to navigate inflationary pressures while maintaining margins, which leads me to Ambev’s Profitability, a key strength in its Quant Factor Grades.

ABEV’s Net Income and Levered FCF margins both demonstrate its sector dominance as the metrics are each more than four times its consumer staples peers. The company’s ability to manage input costs and pass select pricing through to consumers enhances its competitive moat. In an environment where consumers trade down to value-oriented staples, Ambev’s strong regional brands and streamlined cost structure are major advantages that can keep its sector-crushing performance momentum going in 2026.

From here, this consumer value orientation sets the stage for distribution-centric staples like UNFI, which play a key role in ensuring shelf availability across food retail.

4. United Natural Foods, Inc. (UNFI)

  • Market Capitalization: $2.28B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 01/29/2026): 4 out of 179

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 01/29/2026): 1 out of 7

  • Sector: Consumer Staples

  • Industry: Food Distributors

United Natural Foods is a leading food distributor supplying grocery stores, natural food outlets, and other retailers with a broad range of staples products. As supply chains continue to normalize after pandemic and tariff disruptions, UNFI’s scale in distribution and logistics has translated into renewed operational leverage. The company benefits from steady demand for essential groceries and natural products, while increasing retailer emphasis on streamlined inventory and cost efficiency plans, to UNFI’s strengths. To illustrate, I’ll begin with UNFI’s Quant Factor Grade strength in growth.

UNFI’s forward EBITDA Growth of 12.15% is a key metric that demonstrates its sector leadership, as consumer staples peers measure at a median of 4.35%. Even more attractive, forward EPS Long Term Growth (3-5Y CAGR) is expected at 79.34%, more than 11 times the sector median. UNFI’s role as a wholesale backbone gives it unique visibility into consumer purchasing trends, which drive its tailored strategies to boost margins, potentially supporting its recent performance momentum.

This profit-driven strength and defensiveness caps the individual stock section and leads naturally into a broader sector wrap about resilience and relative value appeal in the 2026 macro environment.

Conclusion: Consumer Staples Can Balance a Mixed 2026 Outlook

Collectively, these four Quant Strong Buys in the consumer staples sector illustrate the market segment’s defensive appeal and differentiated growth potential as 2026 unfolds. From Village Farms’ vertically integrated produce and wellness portfolio to Nature’s Sunshine Products’ direct-to-consumer supplements, Ambev’s beverage leadership, and United Natural Foods’ distribution backbone, each offers a unique angle on staples’ resiliency in uncertain times. While macro conditions, such as evolving consumer confidence and policy support remain mixed, staples’ relative stability and essential nature make them compelling considerations for diversified portfolios.

We have many stocks with strong buy recommendations, and you can filter them using Stock Screens to suit your specific investment objectives. Alternatively, Alpha Picks might be ideal if you're interested in two monthly stock picks of the top 'strong buy' quant stocks. Seeking Alpha’s quant ratings and investment research tools help to ensure you are furnished with the best resources to make informed investment decisions while taking the emotion out of investing.

If you’re looking for more frequent investment ideas, we launched the PRO Quant Portfolio in June, 2025. It’s a weekly rebalanced selection of our Top 30 Quant Strong Buy stocks, spanning multiple regions and market caps. Happy Investing!

data.title: 3 Reasonable Market Predictions For February
data.description:
Friday will mark the last trading day in the first month of 2026. The markets have remained quite resilient despite numerous concerns around the economy and equities. In January, Greenland and Minneapolis became two unexpected sources of headlines and turmoil.

A bit of short-lived volatility triggered by ever-changing tariff policies was a theme during January as well. The massive winter storm Fern brought snow across most of the nation and is likely to ding Q1 GDP growth by at least one half of one percent. Especially as another large winter storm is expected to hit a good chunk of the country this weekend. Despite this, we are coming to the end of the first frame of the New Year with equities trading at or near all-time highs.

So, what lies ahead for investors for the shortest month of the year? Three market predictions for February follow below.

1. The AI Story Becomes More Problematic

While equities overall are nicely up to start 2026, the stocks of some of the tech giants that have powered the rally over the past three-plus years have gotten dinged to start the new year. Oracle (ORCL) looks like it will be down more than 10% in January, and Microsoft (MSFT) is heading to a more than 10% loss for the month as well.

Investors are starting to ask pertinent questions around some of the core pillars of the AI Narrative on a more frequent basis. Questions around how the huge new electricity demands from the massive AI data center buildout will be met and whether AI-related revenues will rise enough in coming years to even begin to justify the hundreds of billions of dollars being invested annually for the AI infrastructure buildout.

You can see how these trends are playing across the ecosystem this week. Microsoft is seeing its stock trade significantly down on Thursday despite posting Q4 results that beat both top- and bottom-line expectations. Mister Softie's cloud revenue was up 29% year-over-year and came in at over $50 billion. Non-GAAP income was $38.9 billion for the quarter. However, on a GAAP basis, net income came in at $30.9 billion, $8 billion less. Microsoft's non-GAAP results exclude the impact from investments in OpenAI; it should be noted. Significant additional investment in OpenAI (OPENAI) may also be in the offing as well.

Meta Platforms (META) noted in their just-posted fourth-quarter results that it expects to nearly double capex spending due to AI in FY2026 to a range of $115 billion to $135 billion. Capex for Q4 came in at $21.4 billion, up $7 billion from the same period a year ago. It should be noted that this investment will generate little revenue, at least over the near and potentially medium term. To put in perspective, all U.S. AI revenues are not expected to broach $100 billion industry-wide until FY2027.

The company also added $6.02 billion in the quarter to the $73 billion in cumulative losses in the company's virtual reality and Metaverse efforts, aka Reality Labs. A huge investment that has not panned out to this point proves even tech visionaries are not omnipotent.

As I noted in an earlier article, Morgan Stanley this week slashed its price target on Oracle (ORCL) by one-third. Morgan Stanley noted Oracle's “rapid buildout of artificial intelligence infrastructure will likely push earnings per share results to fall below expected targets and increase capital expenditures.”

I expect scrutiny around AI capex versus AI revenues to become a much bigger topic in February and the months ahead. Will this be the prick that pops the AI Bubble? As my late father liked to say, “Only the Shadow knows.”

2. Gold Pauses

One of the big stories in January was the huge rally in gold, silver, and other precious metals. Three years ago, gold sold for just under $1,900 an ounce. It broached $5,500 an ounce early on Thursday in what has been a parabolic move this month.

Silver has had a much bigger move here in January. There have been many explanations for these huge moves. The dollar debasement/sell America theme is probably the most cited one at the moment.

Japan has seen a huge spike up in its yields on its debt, with 30-year yields hitting their highest levels ever and 10-year yields moving to their highest threshold since 1999. With a debt-to-GDP ratio of roughly twice that of the U.S. and the second oldest population of any country, this has put a renewed focus on sovereign debt across the G-20. Rising concerns around Japan was one of my three market predictions for January; it should be noted.

A piece on Seeking Alpha Thursday provided a good summary of what is driving gold prices higher from Seeking Alpha analyst Frank Holmes:

Multiple tailwinds - runaway debt, Fed credibility concerns, global central bank buying - are fueling a 'perfect storm' for gold prices.

There are certainly good reasons for rising precious metal prices. However, an approximate 60% move in silver and a better than 25% rise in gold in January have the feel of blow-off tops. I would also be concerned about the potential impacts on counterparties from those funds/investors that have been on the wrong side of these moves.

My second prediction for February is these moves in precious metals will at least pause in the coming month. I also would not be surprised to see significant profit-taking given the sharp moves up across the precious metal complex.

3. Volatility Spikes

I think spiking commodity prices are a prelude to much higher volatility in the equity markets in the month ahead. The last time precious metals had this sort of year-over-year surge was in 1979-1980. This was in a stagflationary period when the PE on the S&P 500 was in the high single digits and the misery index was a key theme within the economy and on the news.

February also could open with another partial government shutdown and/or military action in Iran. The latter being one reason oil prices are moving up sharply this week. Despite these concerns, the S&P VIX Index (VIX) has been quite calm since seeing a huge surge during the rollout of “reciprocal tariff” policies in early April. We have had a couple of spikes above 20 since then, but all of these have quickly dissipated. Including the one I predicted for January.

My last prediction for February is that this market calm will be upset at least once this month and the VIX will broach the 30 level. In addition, equity markets will see at least one day when the NASDAQ loses more than 2.4%, as it did on Jan. 20 during the peak of the Greenland worries.

And this wraps up my three market predictions for the coming month. The markets continue to trade at extreme valuation levels. Equities have remained resilient in January, and complacency continues to be a core pillar of the current investment landscape. I doubt this lasts much longer.

As Sergeant Phil Esterhaus of Hill Street Blues famously quipped, “Let’s be careful out there.”
